entry.title: Building a neural network from scratch: from one neuron to solving XOR
entry.content:
I wanted to use PyTorch, but before I let a framework do backprop for
me I wanted to make sure I understood what backprop is. So I wrote a
neural network in pure Python — no NumPy, no PyTorch — and built it up
from the smallest possible piece. One neuron. Then two. Then a whole
layer. Then a network.

This is the write-up. The surprise at the end: the whole thing, the
part that people call “how neural networks learn”, is just the chain
rule applied sensibly. Nothing hidden. Nothing magic.

Step 1: one neuron

A neuron is literally two lines of code:

Given weights $(w_1, w_2, b)$ you can hand-tune a neuron to separate
two clouds of 2D points. Here’s what I got trying different weights on
blue-around-$(-2,-2)$ versus red-around-$(2,2)$:

$w_1$$w_2$$b$accuracy
1.01.00.0100.0%
1.01.0-2.099.0%
0.50.50.0100.0%
-1.0-1.00.00.0% (flipped)

For two cleanly-separated clouds, a human can find good weights by
guessing. Three dials is tractable. But by the time you have 17
parameters (a 2-4-1 network), there’s no way. You need the computer
to find them.

Step 2: gradient descent

Loss = mean squared error between the neuron’s output and the true
label. You compute the gradient of the loss with respect to each
parameter, then nudge each parameter in the direction that reduces
loss. Repeat for many epochs.

The gradients are just derivatives, applied layer by layer:

Three things to notice:

  1. d_sigmoid = pred * (1 - pred) — the derivative of sigmoid
    conveniently uses the output of the neuron, not the input.
    That’s a standard trick that saves recomputation.
  2. Each line is the chain rule: dL/dwi = (dL/dpred) * (dpred/dz) * (dz/dwi).
    The only “interesting” part is (dz/dwi) = xi.
  3. This code alone, with one neuron and no hidden layer, already finds
    100% accurate weights for the two-cloud problem.

Step 3: adding a hidden layer (and deriving backprop)

To solve hard classification problems — ones where a single straight
line can’t separate the classes — I need a hidden layer. The classic
example is XOR:

You cannot draw a line that separates the 1s from the 0s.

The 2-layer network looks like:

with two weight matrices, $W_1$ and $W_2$. To train it, I need
$\partial L / \partial W_1$ and $\partial L / \partial W_2$. The chain
rule, spelled out:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \hat{y}} &= 2(\hat{y} - y) \
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial z_{\text{out}}} &= \hat{y}(1 - \hat{y}) \
\frac{\partial z_{\text{out}}}{\partial W_2} &= h \
\frac{\partial z_{\text{out}}}{\partial h} &= W_2 \
\frac{\partial h}{\partial z_{\text{hidden}}} &= h(1 - h) \
\frac{\partial z_{\text{hidden}}}{\partial W_1} &= x
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiply these together in the right order (called the backward
pass) and you get the gradient for every parameter. That’s
backpropagation. That is the entirety of “how neural networks learn”.

In Python:

Step 4: verify the derivation before trusting it

Before I trained anything, I checked my gradients numerically.

For any parameter $w$:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} \approx \frac{L(w + \epsilon) - L(w - \epsilon)}{2\epsilon}
$$

So I can run forward() twice with a tiny perturbation, divide, and
compare against the gradient my backward pass computed. Here’s the
output for the first few weights:

Relative error under $10^{-7}$. The analytic gradient matches the
finite-difference gradient to about ten digits. Backprop is implemented
correctly.

This is the single most valuable habit I picked up from the project.
Whenever I implement a gradient anywhere, I gradient-check it on a
small example. Bugs in gradient code are silent — they look like “the
model isn’t learning well” — and gradient-check catches them instantly.

Step 5: solve XOR

2-4-1 network, sigmoid everywhere, lr = 2.0, 10,000 epochs. Training
loss:

Three different hidden-layer sizes. Things to notice:

  • 2-2-1 plateaus for about 500 epochs before dropping. That’s a
    “symmetry-breaking” period: with only 2 hidden neurons, the network
    has to escape a local-minimum-ish region before it finds a layout that
    works.
  • 2-4-1 and 2-8-1 drop right away. More hidden neurons means more
    ways to stumble onto a good configuration.
  • All three reach ~$10^{-4}$ loss eventually, but 2-8-1 does it fastest
    and most smoothly.

Final predictions (2-4-1):

inputtargetnetwork output
(0, 0)00.0033
(0, 1)10.9921
(1, 0)10.9923
(1, 1)00.0117

XOR solved. Better than perfect — the network isn’t just right, it’s
confident.

What the hidden neurons actually learned

Looking at the trained weights of the 2-4-1 network:

Each hidden neuron learned a line in the (x1, x2) plane. Four lines.
The output layer then combines them nonlinearly — “big if hidden[0] is
big AND hidden[1] is small AND …” etc — to carve out the XOR pattern.

This is why a single neuron can’t solve XOR but a 2-layer network can:
one line can’t separate XOR’s 1s from its 0s, but two lines composed by
a second layer absolutely can. Minsky and Papert proved in 1969 that a
single-layer perceptron is stuck, which nearly killed neural-network
research for a decade. The fix — stacking layers — sounds obvious in
retrospect; the hard part was getting backpropagation right so you
could actually train the deeper networks.

A harder problem: concentric rings

To make sure the NN class generalized, I threw a harder 2D problem at
it — points arranged in two concentric rings, inner radius ~1 (label 0)
and outer radius ~3 (label 1). No straight line separates them, and
even two lines can’t.

A 2-8-1 network with sigmoid activations learns a curved decision
boundary — a ring of #s surrounding a central empty region. That’s a
nonlinear shape emerging from a pile of linear pieces.

What I’d change next

  • ReLU instead of sigmoid. Sigmoid saturates: far from zero its
    derivative is close to zero, so gradients shrink as you go deeper.
    ReLU (max(0, x)) has derivative 1 wherever it’s active, which is
    why every modern deep network uses it.
  • Cross-entropy loss for classification. Gives bigger gradients
    when the model is badly wrong.
  • NumPy. My loops are Python-level. Vectorizing the forward and
    backward pass with NumPy would be a ~100x speedup for free, without
    changing any of the math.
  • A real dataset. Still 2D input in my experiments; next step is
    MNIST (784-dimensional input, 10-class output). My pure-Python
    implementation would be way too slow, but that’s what NumPy is for.

The takeaway

Before this project, “the network learns by backpropagation” was a
black box to me. After writing every line by hand and watching the
gradient check pass, it’s… not a black box. It’s a for-loop.

PyTorch is still amazing — it does this for you, it does it in
CUDA, it handles arbitrary computation graphs. But now I know what
it’s doing. Which means when it breaks, I’ll have some hope of
debugging it.

Code for this post:
github.com/jerryzhenyuliu/neural-networks-from-scratch

entry.title: Why does the secant method converge at the golden ratio?
entry.content:
I was working through a numerical-methods experiment comparing three
ways to solve $f(x) = 0$: bisection, Newton’s method, and the secant
method. I expected the textbook story — bisection is slow, Newton is
fast, secant is somewhere in between.

Then I measured it and the secant method’s convergence order came out
to about 1.618.

That’s the golden ratio. That’s $\varphi$. That’s the number from
Fibonacci and pentagons and sunflowers.

What is it doing in a root-finding algorithm?

The three methods

Bisection halves the interval each step — that’s linear convergence.
The error at step $n+1$ is roughly half the error at step $n$.

Newton’s method replaces the true function with its tangent line.
It’s the gold standard: under nice conditions its error squares
each step. The number of correct digits roughly doubles every
iteration. That’s quadratic convergence — order 2.

The secant method is “Newton’s method, but for people who don’t have
a formula for $f’(x)$”. It approximates the derivative using a finite
difference between the two most recent points. You’d think it’d be
somewhere between linear (order 1) and quadratic (order 2).

And it is. Specifically: it’s order $\varphi \approx 1.618$.

The measurement

Solving $\cos(x) = x$ starting from $x_0 = 0, x_1 = 1$. The true root
is about $0.7390851332$. Here’s the error at each secant step:

| step | $x$ | error $|e_n|$ |
|——|—–|—–|
| 1 | 0.6850734… | $5.4 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| 2 | 0.7362989… | $2.8 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 3 | 0.7391112… | $2.6 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| 4 | 0.7390851352… | $2.0 \times 10^{-9}$ |
| 5 | 0.7390851332… | $5.6 \times 10^{-14}$ |
| 6 | 0.7390851332… | $< 10^{-15}$ |

Watch what happens to the number of correct digits per step:

  • step 1 → 2: goes from ~1 to ~3 digits (gained ~2)
  • step 2 → 3: goes from ~3 to ~5 digits (gained ~2)
  • step 3 → 4: goes from ~5 to ~9 digits (gained ~4)
  • step 4 → 5: goes from ~9 to ~14 digits (gained ~5)

Compare to Newton’s on the same problem, which doubles the digit
count each step; and bisection, which adds about 0.3 digits per
step. Secant lives in between. But not in the middle — it pulls toward
Newton, which is where the golden ratio comes from.

The dashed line is the prediction $|e_{n+1}| \sim C \cdot |e_n|^{\varphi}$,
and the measurement tracks it closely.

Where $\varphi$ comes from

Here’s the reasoning. For a smooth function $f$ with a simple root $r$,
you can show (with some Taylor-expansion algebra) that the secant-method
error satisfies, in the limit:

$$
|e_{n+1}| \approx C \cdot |e_n| \cdot |e_{n-1}|
$$

That is: the new error depends on the previous two errors multiplied
together. (Newton’s method doesn’t have this coupling — each step only
depends on the current error.)

Now suppose $|e_n| \sim K \cdot \alpha^n$ for some constants $K$ and $\alpha$.
Substituting:

$$
K \alpha^{n+1} = C \cdot K \alpha^n \cdot K \alpha^{n-1}
$$

Dividing by $K \alpha^{n-1}$:

$$
\alpha^2 = C K \alpha^0 = CK
$$

Hmm, that gave me a constant, not an equation in $\alpha$. Let me try a
more flexible ansatz: $|e_n| \sim K^{\varphi^n}$, which says the error
shrinks “super-exponentially” in $n$, with some exponent that grows. Take
the log of both sides of $|e_{n+1}| \sim |e_n| \cdot |e_{n-1}|$:

$$
\log |e_{n+1}| \sim \log |e_n| + \log |e_{n-1}|
$$

So if I let $L_n = -\log |e_n|$ (positive when the error is small), then:

$$
L_{n+1} = L_n + L_{n-1}
$$

That’s the Fibonacci recurrence. And the asymptotic growth rate of
Fibonacci numbers is $\varphi = (1 + \sqrt{5})/2$. So:

$$
L_{n+1} \sim \varphi \cdot L_n
$$

which translates back to:

$$
|e_{n+1}| \sim |e_n|^{\varphi}
$$

Convergence order = $\varphi$. The secant method inherits its
convergence rate directly from the Fibonacci recurrence.

Why this is the thing I remember most from this project

I’ve seen $\varphi$ in dozens of places. Pentagons. Sunflower seeds.
Fibonacci ratios. Self-similar pineapples. The Parthenon, if you
believe the aesthetics people.

Every one of those is a structural appearance of $\varphi$ — it’s
there because the geometry or the recurrence enforces it. But this was
the first time I’d seen it emerge from an error analysis — as a
consequence of which two previous errors the algorithm depends on, and
nothing else. No geometry. Nothing you could draw as a spiral. Just a
recurrence.

The same number that describes how leaves arrange on a stem describes
how fast the secant method tracks down a root of $\cos(x) = x$. That’s
a lot more surprising than any of the usual $\varphi$ encounters.

Practical corollary

So which root-finder to use in practice?

  • Bisection: slow (~40 steps to hit $10^{-12}$), but cannot fail
    as long as $f(a)$ and $f(b)$ have opposite signs. Use it when you
    care about robustness more than speed.
  • Newton: fastest (~6 steps), but needs $f’(x)$ explicitly, and can
    diverge or land on the wrong root if your initial guess is poor.
  • Secant: ~8 steps, no derivative needed. Its robustness is between
    the other two. In most engineering code you’ll see secant-like methods
    (Brent’s method is a hybrid) chosen over Newton precisely because
    computing a derivative is often impractical.

And next time someone asks me “where does the golden ratio show up”, I
have one more answer.

Code for this experiment:
github.com/jerryzhenyuliu/computational-math/blob/main/05_root_finding.py

entry.title: A median-of-5 pivot for quicksort — the result I'm most proud of
entry.content:
The assignment for my sorting project was to implement and benchmark six
classical sorting algorithms. I did. But part-way through, I got
curious about something: random-pivot quicksort works well on average,
but every single run is a roll of the dice. Can I do better without
giving up the simplicity?

This post is about the thing I tried, and the result (~18% fewer
comparisons, ~12% faster wall time on random input). It’s my favorite
outcome from the whole project.

The problem with a random pivot

Quicksort’s speed depends almost entirely on one thing: how balanced the
partition is. If the pivot happens to land near the median of the
subarray, you split it roughly in half and the recursion is O(log n) deep.
If the pivot lands near the extreme, you get a 1-vs-(n-1) split, the
recursion is O(n) deep, and the whole thing degrades to O(n²).

Random pivot is a pragmatic defense — it prevents an adversarial input
from being a worst case, but any single run can still get unlucky.

On average this is great. On any specific input, there’s variance.

The idea: sample more, pick the middle

Median-of-three is the textbook fix — pick the first, middle, and last
elements, take the median of those three as the pivot. It’s cheap (a
few extra comparisons per call) and noticeably more stable than random.

I wanted to see how far this goes. What if I sampled five positions
instead?

The five positions are evenly spaced across the subarray. I sort those
five by value, take the one with the median value, and use its position
as the pivot.

The cost per call is constant — five reads plus sorting five items,
which is some fixed number of comparisons. The asymptotic complexity
is still O(n log n); I’m not going to change that. But the split
quality improves, and that compounds at every level of the recursion.

The measurement

Uniform random input, same seed, single run (not averaged over many —
this is what the first experimental look felt like):

nrandom pivot3-way pivotmedian-of-5
100.06 ms, 25 cmps0.08 ms, 22 cmps0.06 ms, 21 cmps
1001.68 ms, 667 cmps1.11 ms, 655 cmps1.25 ms, 550 cmps
1,00011.96 ms, 11,140 cmps14.03 ms, 11,266 cmps9.48 ms, 8,762 cmps
10,000132.2 ms, 154,674 cmps148.8 ms, 154,144 cmps127.1 ms, 125,576 cmps
100,0001394 ms, 2,016,270 cmps1566 ms, 1,921,818 cmps1230 ms, 1,641,453 cmps

At n = 100,000, median-of-5 does 19% fewer comparisons than random
pivot (2.02M → 1.64M) and finishes ~12% faster in wall time
(1394 ms → 1230 ms).

The gap actually widens as n grows — because each recursion level’s
savings compound with every other level.

What I think happened

Math says: if I pick 5 random samples from an input and take their
median, the pivot’s expected deviation from the true median is
substantially smaller than with 1 or 3 samples. The variance of the
sample median shrinks with more samples.

Concretely: a random pivot has ~1/n probability of being the worst
possible choice. Median-of-5 has ~(1/n)⁵ probability of all five samples
being bad simultaneously. The tail of worst cases gets dramatically
thinner even though the constant cost per call doesn’t change.

So what I’m buying is lower variance, not a faster asymptotic. That’s
reflected in the results: median-of-5 doesn’t beat random pivot by a
lot, but it beats it consistently, and it rules out the rare disaster.

What the real libraries do

After I was proud of this, I looked up what CPython actually does. List
sort is Timsort, which isn’t quicksort at all — it’s a hybrid of merge
sort and insertion sort that exploits pre-existing order in real data.
So Python doesn’t face the quicksort-pivot problem.

But Java’s Arrays.sort for primitives is dual-pivot quicksort —
picks two pivots per call, and partitions into three regions
(less, between, greater). The idea is the same: spend a bit more per
call to get more balanced splits.

So my median-of-5 is the amateur version of a real production technique.
The core pattern — “pay constant extra cost per call to reduce the tail
variance” — is the same.

Things I didn’t try (yet)

  • Introsort: run quicksort normally, and switch to heapsort if
    recursion depth exceeds log₂(n). That provably bounds the worst
    case at O(n log n), which my version doesn’t do.
  • Quickselect-based pivot: find the true median in O(n) time (there’s
    an algorithm). Too expensive for most subarrays, but might be worth it
    near the root of the recursion.
  • Timing against list.sort(): Python’s built-in will crush all of
    mine because it’s implemented in C. I haven’t yet — partly because it
    would be humbling and partly because the comparison only really makes
    sense at the algorithmic level, not the implementation level.

The part that surprised me

I expected median-of-5 to be “maybe slightly better” than random pivot.
19% fewer comparisons at n = 100,000 is more than I expected.

I also didn’t expect 3-way partition to be slower than random-pivot
quicksort on uniform random input — the yellow line in the chart above
is consistently above the red. 3-way is a big win on duplicate-heavy
input (see my previous post),
but on uniform random the extra partition bookkeeping costs more than
the infrequent duplicates save.

This is what I mean by wanting to see things rather than read about
them. “Algorithm A is better than algorithm B” is almost always true
only under specific conditions, and the conditions are usually more
important than the algorithm.

Code for this post: github.com/jerryzhenyuliu/sorting-algorithms/blob/main/quick_median5.py

entry.title: Six sorting algorithms, and the O(n²) gap that hit harder than I expected
entry.content:
I just finished my first “proper” CS project: implement six classical
sorting algorithms and actually measure how they stack up. I knew going
in that “O(n log n) beats O(n²)”. The textbook says so. But I wanted to
see the gap with my own eyes, not just know it as a fact.

Seeing the numbers on my own laptop turned out to be a different kind of
understanding.

The lineup

Six algorithms, same function signature so the experiment could treat
them uniformly:

Similar single-file implementations for insertion sort, merge sort,
quicksort (with random pivot and with 3-way Dutch-flag partition), and
counting sort. Every one of them returns (sorted_list, comparison_count) —
so I could count comparisons separately from wall time.

Six input distributions

The textbook complexity is for “random” input, but I wanted to probe the
edges:

  1. Uniform random — the default
  2. Already sorted
  3. Reverse sorted
  4. Almost sorted (5% of elements swapped)
  5. Many duplicates (values only in [0..9])
  6. Block-sorted (sorted chunks concatenated in shuffled order)

Data generation uses a fixed seed so runs are reproducible.

What the numbers look like

I ran each (algorithm × distribution × n) seven times and kept the
median. Here’s the big-picture log-log plot on uniform random input:

The lines you want to look at:

  • Red and yellow (bubble, insertion) — steeper. Each decade of n adds
    two decades of time. That’s slope ≈ 2, which is O(n²) on log-log.
  • Green and blue (merge, quick) — shallower. Each decade of n adds
    slightly more than one decade of time. Slope ≈ 1, consistent with
    O(n log n).
  • Purple (counting sort) — almost flat at first, then a sudden
    hop. More on that below.

The gap in raw numbers

Time at n = 10,000 on uniform random input:

algorithmtime (ms)comparisons
bubble10,84049,991,840
insertion4,34425,059,528
merge33120,370
quick (random)45150,539
counting280

Bubble sort took almost eleven seconds on a list of ten thousand
integers. Merge sort took 33 milliseconds. That’s a factor of ~330.
“O(n²) is slower” stops being an abstract phrase when one version is
literally waiting for lunch to finish and the other one is done before
you blink.

The comparison counts tell the same story without the noise of machine
timing. Merge sort does ~120K comparisons on that input; bubble sort
does 50 million.

The interesting edge: input shape matters a lot

Here’s the same six algorithms across all six input distributions, at
n = 1000:

A few things jumped out:

Bubble sort has one superpower

On already-sorted input, bubble sort does one pass. A single sweep of
O(n) comparisons, the early-termination flag never trips, it exits.
On that one specific input, the thing everyone calls “the worst sort”
beats everything except counting sort. Useful reminder that “best” is
never a property of an algorithm alone — it’s always algorithm times
input.

Quicksort’s weakness is duplicates, not size

The “many duplicates” column is where I caught something real. Normal
random-pivot quicksort uses roughly as many comparisons on duplicate-heavy
data as on random data — each duplicate still has to decide “less than
or greater than pivot” and lands in one of the two sides. But 3-way
quicksort (Dutch flag partition) drops the duplicates into an “equal”
middle bucket in one pass, and the recursion skips over them entirely.
On all-equal input, that’s O(n) instead of O(n log n).

This is the reason most modern sort implementations use some form of
3-way partition — they get linear time on highly skewed real-world data
almost for free.

Counting sort wins only when it wins

Counting sort is O(n + k) where k is the value range. At n = 100,000
and k = 100, it’s three times faster than merge sort. At k = 10,000
it’s slower. At k = 1,000,000 it runs out of memory trying to allocate
a million-element bucket array. So it’s not a “better” algorithm — it’s
a different tool that’s useful under specific conditions (small,
known integer range).

The part I thought I understood but didn’t

I’d read “O(n log n) beats O(n²) for large n” a dozen times before
writing any of this. But reading it and watching bubble sort take
11 seconds on your own machine while merge sort finishes in a blink are
completely different experiences.

There’s also a subtler thing: the constants matter. At n = 10 or n = 100,
bubble sort can actually beat merge sort because merge sort’s recursion
overhead dominates for tiny inputs. Real-world sort implementations
(CPython’s Timsort, Java’s dual-pivot quicksort) hybridize — they
switch to insertion sort for small subarrays. That only makes sense
once you’ve measured the crossover point yourself.

What’s next

I’m writing a separate post about the other half of this project: I
tried to invent my own pivot-selection strategy for quicksort, called
“median of 5”, and it ended up saving ~18% of comparisons on random
input. That one’s more fun — I’ll link it here
when it’s up.

All the code for this post is at
github.com/jerryzhenyuliu/sorting-algorithms.

entry.title: Hello World
entry.content:
This is the first post on what I hope will become a long-running notebook.

I’m a high school student who spends a lot of time coding and reading about
algorithms, maths, and machine learning. Most of what I learn lives in
half-finished Jupyter notebooks and Markdown files on my laptop — this
blog is an attempt to tidy some of it up, write it out properly, and share it.

What I plan to post

  • Project write-ups — when I finish something worth writing up
    (a sorting-algorithm benchmark, a neural network from scratch,
    a numerical-methods comparison), I’ll turn it into a post here.
  • Short notes — things I found interesting while reading a paper
    or watching a lecture. Not polished articles, just “here’s what
    clicked for me”.
  • Build diaries — occasional longer posts when I take on a bigger
    project and want to record what I tried and what failed.

Currently working on

Three projects, all in pure Python, all landing on GitHub this week:

  1. Sorting algorithms
    — six classical sorts, experiments on six different input distributions.
    I also tried designing a median-of-5 pivot variant for quicksort;
    it saves ~18% of comparisons on random input.

  2. Computational math
    — Monte Carlo estimation of pi and e, the Prime Number Theorem,
    Goldbach’s conjecture, and three methods for solving equations
    numerically. The most surprising thing I learned: the secant
    method’s convergence order is the golden ratio.

  3. Neural networks from scratch
    — building up from a single neuron to a multi-layer network.
    No NumPy, no PyTorch. Every derivative by hand, every gradient
    verified with finite differences.

Write-ups of each will land on this blog over the coming weeks.

Why blog at all

Two reasons. First, writing forces me to notice when I don’t actually
understand something — I can fool myself in my head but not on paper.
Second, almost every good resource I’ve found online was written by
someone who wasn’t famous or formally trained, just curious and willing
to put their notes up. That’s the kind of resource I’d like to
contribute to eventually.

More to come.
